title: RavenClaude comfort posture
description: Categorical permission-autonomy scale for Claude Code. Edited via this plugin's dashboard.html Settings tab; saved to .ravenclaude/comfort-posture.yaml in the consumer's project. The /set-posture skill translates this YAML into .claude/settings.json permission rules.

_level_descriptions.deny: Hard block. Claude refuses the action even if asked. Use for things you NEVER want to happen in this project (e.g., production writes from a dev workspace).
_level_descriptions.always-ask: Confirm every single time. Claude pauses and asks before each action, even repeat actions.
_level_descriptions.mostly-ask: Confirm except for obviously-safe cases. Claude asks for anything ambiguous; allows the cheap, well-known patterns.
_level_descriptions.default: Confirm on ambiguity. Claude uses judgment: cheap reads flow, risky writes ask. Matches Claude Code's built-in READONLY_COMMANDS partition.
_level_descriptions.mostly-allow: Allow except for risky cases. Claude acts on most patterns; asks only when the shape is unusual or destructive.
_level_descriptions.productive: Allow without asking for any pattern Claude can identify as safe. Asks only when escalation is required by the engine.
_level_descriptions.yolo: Never ask. Claude acts on every pattern in this category. Maximum velocity; minimum oversight. Useful inside throwaway sandboxes.

cautious._description: Sets every category to always-ask. Claude pauses before every non-trivial action. Maximum oversight, slowest velocity. Recommended starting point per Koch et al.'s 'OK blindness' finding.

default._description: Sets every category to default. Claude uses judgment: cheap reads flow, risky writes ask. Balanced posture.

productive._description: WARNING: Sets every category to productive. Claude acts without asking for any pattern it can identify as safe. THIS INCLUDES git push, npm/pip install, arbitrary code execution (python -c), and writes outside the project (~/.ssh, /etc, etc.). Use only when you trust Claude to act unattended.

global_default.title: Global default
global_default.description: Used for any category not explicitly set below. Apply a preset to set this and all categories at once.
global_default.x-example: If you set this to 'mostly-ask', Claude will ask before nearly every action regardless of category — unless you set a specific category lower.

categories.title: Per-category overrides
categories.description: Each category's autonomy level. Override the global default per category.

file_read_project.title: Read files in this project
file_read_project.description: ls, cat, grep, reading source files inside the current project directory.
file_read_project.x-example:
Examples: `ls src/`, `cat README.md`, `grep -r TODO`, opening a file in the editor.

Most users want this on 'default' or 'productive' — reading source files is cheap and you usually want Claude to look around freely. Set to 'mostly-ask' if your project has files Claude shouldn't routinely scan (e.g., a checked-in keystore).

file_edit_project.title: Edit files in this project
file_edit_project.description: Write, Edit, MultiEdit on files inside the current project.
file_edit_project.x-example:
Examples: `Write('src/new.py', ...)`, `Edit('package.json', ...)`, editing a Markdown doc.

If you want to review every change before it lands, set this to 'always-ask' or 'mostly-ask'. 'Productive' lets Claude refactor or generate freely; the diff is visible after the fact via git.

file_read_global.title: Read files outside this project
file_read_global.x-example:
Examples: `cat ~/.ssh/config`, `cat ~/.aws/credentials`, `head /etc/hosts`.

This is almost always set to 'always-ask' or 'deny'. Files outside your project directory often contain secrets (SSH keys, AWS creds, browser cookies). Only relax this if you have a specific reason.

file_edit_global.title: Edit files outside this project
file_edit_global.description: Writing to your home directory or system paths.
file_edit_global.x-example:
Examples: `Write('~/.profile', ...)`, `echo X > /etc/hosts`, editing a config in `~/Library`.

Keep this on 'always-ask' or 'deny' unless you have a specific reason to let Claude touch system or home-dir files. CVE-2026-39861 shows what goes wrong when sandboxed code writes outside the workspace.

shell_readonly.title: Read-only shell commands
shell_readonly.description: ls, cat, git status, grep, find. The cheap, no-side-effect stuff.
shell_readonly.x-example:
Examples: `ls`, `cat file.txt`, `git status`, `git log`, `grep -r foo`, `find . -name '*.md'`.

Claude Code already auto-allows many of these (the built-in READONLY_COMMANDS list). 'Productive' or 'yolo' means "never ask for read-only shell" — recommended unless you specifically want every command logged.

shell_local_mutate.title: Local mutations
shell_local_mutate.description: mkdir, mv, cp, git commit. Changes filesystem locally; doesn't talk to a server.
shell_local_mutate.x-example:
Examples: `mkdir src/new-feature`, `mv old.py new.py`, `git commit -m '...'`, `git checkout -b feature/x`.

Local mutations are recoverable (`git reflog` exists, files can be restored). 'Default' or 'mostly-allow' is usually right — Claude can move things around but flags ambiguous moves.

shell_remote_mutate.title: Remote mutations
shell_remote_mutate.description: git push, gh pr create, gh pr comment. Anything that publishes to a shared system.
shell_remote_mutate.x-example:
Examples: `git push`, `gh pr create`, `gh pr comment`, `npm publish`, `aws s3 sync`.

These go to shared systems where the change is visible to other people (or paying customers). Most users keep this on 'always-ask' or 'mostly-ask' to preserve the review step. 'Productive' only after Claude has earned that trust on your project.

shell_code_exec.title: Arbitrary code execution
shell_code_exec.description: python -c, node -e, bash -c, eval — any way to run code that wasn't pre-written.
shell_code_exec.x-example:
Examples: `python -c 'import os; os.system(...)'`, `node -e 'process.exit(0)'`, `bash -c 'curl ... | sh'`.

This is the category most often abused in security incidents. 'Always-ask' or 'mostly-ask' is the safe default — running arbitrary code from a prompt is the shortest path to RCE if anything goes wrong with the prompt itself.

shell_package_install.title: Package installs
shell_package_install.description: npm install, pip install, apt install, brew install.
shell_package_install.x-example:
Examples: `npm install foo`, `pip install bar`, `apt install -y baz`, `brew install qux`.

Package installs are higher-risk than they look: many run install scripts with full shell access on your machine. Most users keep this on 'always-ask' so you can review the package name before it lands.

network_read.title: Web reads
network_read.description: WebFetch on known documentation domains; HTTP GET requests.
network_read.x-example:
Examples: `WebFetch('https://docs.anthropic.com/...')`, `curl https://api.github.com/...`, looking up an MDN article.

'Default' or 'mostly-allow' is usually right — Claude needs to look things up to give grounded answers. The risk is limited to whatever the response contains (which Claude has to read carefully anyway).

network_write.title: Web writes
network_write.description: POST/PUT/DELETE to web endpoints; anything that mutates remote state.
network_write.x-example:
Examples: `curl -X POST https://api.../webhook`, `gh api PATCH /repos/.../issues/123`, posting to a Slack webhook.

Network writes can have side effects (charged API calls, posted messages, triggered workflows). Keep this on 'always-ask' or 'mostly-ask' unless you're in a sandbox.

mcp_tools.title: MCP server tools
mcp_tools.description: Tools provided by connected MCP servers.
mcp_tools.x-example:
Examples: `microsoft_learn.docs_search(...)`, `uber.get_estimates(...)`, `google_drive.list_files(...)`.

MCP tool trust is server-by-server. This setting is the GLOBAL default; you can still configure per-server trust in your Claude Code config. 'Default' is usually right — Claude asks before invoking any MCP tool, lets you pin trusted servers individually.
